paragraphs.text: “Isaac was old, and his eyes were dim… and he said… to Esau: Take me venison: and make me savoury meat… that I may eat, that my soul may bless thee before I die” (Gen 27:1, 3–4). Esau went hunting. Hearing this, Rebekah advises Jacob to go to his father, lest Esau receive the birthright contrary to what God had said: “the elder shall serve the younger.” But he did not listen to her, because he feared to receive a curse instead of a blessing. But when Rebekah said: “Upon me be thy curse” (Gen 27:13), then Jacob did as Rebekah commanded. He took the prepared meat, brought it to his father, and said to him: “My father.” And his father asked him: “Who art thou, my son? And he said… to his father: I am Esau… I have done according as thou badest me” (Gen 27:18–19). Isaac doubted, hearing Jacob’s voice. And since he feared lest the blessing and the birthright should pass from Esau to another, he said: “Come near, that I may feel thee” (Gen 27:21). When the deceptive touch of the hands took the false voice for the true, then Jacob received the blessing and went away. Then came Esau, who had prepared the meat, and invited his father to eat. Isaac, seeing that another under the name of Esau had taken hold of the treasure of his blessings, was greatly terrified and said: “Who then is he that hath taken venison, and brought it to me… and I have blessed him? yea, and he shall be blessed” (Gen 27:33). So Isaac could not take back his blessings. First, he knew that thereby was fulfilled the will of the Lord Himself, as had been said to Rebekah. And second, he himself, blessing Jacob, had said: “Cursed be he that curseth thee” (Gen 27:29) — and therefore he feared, that if he should curse Jacob, the curse of his own lips, pronounced against the one whom he had earlier blessed, might turn against himself. “Esau… cried with a great cry” (Gen 27:38), but not because he had lost the spiritual blessings, but because he had been deprived of the good fruits of the blessed land; not because he could no longer be justified, but because he could not have dominion over his brothers; not because he would not inherit immortal life, but because the land of Canaan would not become his portion. And Esau hated Jacob, so that he meant to kill him. Then Rebekah persuaded Jacob to go to Laban’s house, that the brothers in their enmity should not kill each other and that she herself should not lose both her sons at once. She told this to Isaac, and he blessed Jacob and sent him to Haran to take a wife for himself there.
